coredata.dc:title: Synergy between nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots and ZnO nanorods for enhanced hydrogen production
coredata.dc:creator: Yashwanth, H.J.
Rondiya, Sachin R.
Eya, Henry I.
Dzade, Nelson Z.
Phase, Deodatta M.
Dhole, Sanjay D.
Hareesh, K.
coredata.dc:description: We report a facile synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (NPCQDs) anchored on ZnO nanorods to form NPCQDs/ZnO (NPCZ) nanohybrid as efficient and robust photocatalysts for light-driven hydrogen production. The synthesized NPCZ catalyst exhibited improvement in hydrogen production of 417 μmolh−1g−1 under visible light compared to nitrogen doped CQDs/ZnO (NCZ), phosphorus doped CQDs/ZnO (PCZ) and CQDs/ZnO (CZ) nanohybrid photocatalysts. The synergistic interactions between NPCQDs and ZnO nanorods give rise to the formation of additional energy levels, decrease in recombination rate and increase in decay lifetime of photo-generated electron hole pairs, reduced work function, thus the eventual improved hydrogen generation performance in visible region. Further, the experimentally obtained results are consistently corroborated by the first principles Density Functional Theory (DFT), revealing the decrease in bandgap as well as work function and improvement in density of states (DOS) of NPCZ photocatalyst.

ZnO nanorods
Hydrogen generation
DFT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV-Visible absorbance spectroscopy of (a) NPCQD, (b) NPCZ nanohybrids along with ZnO, (c) Tauc’s plot of NPCZ nanohybrids along with ZnO.
(a) & (b) TEM images and (c) HRTEM image of NPCZ nanohybrid. Inset of (c) shows the SAED pattern of NPCZ nanohybrid.

(a) XRD of nanohybrids along with ZnO, (b) XPS survey scan of nanohybrids, high resolution XPS of (c) C 1 s of NPCZ, (d) N 1 s of NPCZ, (e) O 1 s of NPCZ (f) P 2p of NPCZ, (g) Zn 2p of NPCZ along with ZnO, (h) FTIR spectroscopy of nanohybrids along with ZnO, (i) Raman spectroscopy of nanohybrids along with ZnO.
(a) PL; (b) time resolved PL; (c) UPS –Esec edge; and (d) UPS – Fermi edge of CZ, NCZ, PCZ and NPCZ nanohybrids along with ZnO.

Optimized structures of (a1) CQDs/ZnO (CZ) (b1) N-CQDs/ZnO (NCZ), (c) P-CQDZnO (PCZ), and (d1) NP-CQDs/ZnO (NPCZ) nanohybrids. The corresponding differential charge density iso-surfaces (a2-d2), where the cyan and yellow contours indicate electron density decrease and increase by 0.001 e/Å3, respectively. Atomic color: C= gray; N = deep blue; P = orange; H= white, Zn= denim blue, and O= red.

The electrostatic potential (blue continuous line) for (a) CQDs/ZnO (CZ) (b) N-CQDs/ZnO (NCZ), (c) P-CQDZnO (PCZ), and (d) NP-CQDs/ZnO (NPCZ) nanohybrids. The red and black dashed lines represent the vacuum level (Ev) and the Fermi level (EF), respectively. The Φ is the work function.

(a) Photocatalytic hydrogen production of different photocatalysts; (b) Schematic energy band diagram of ZnO and NPCZ photocatalyst; (c) Mott-Schottky plots of ZnO, NPCZ and NPCQD photocatalyst; (d) Stability check for NPCZ photocatalyst.
Schematic representation (a) synthesis of NPCQDs by microwave radiation assisted method; and (b) synthesis of NPCQDs/ZnO nanohybrid by hydrothermal method.
Schematic representation photocatalytic hydrogen production mechanism of NPCZ photocatalyst.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a facile synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (NPCQDs) anchored on ZnO nanorods to form NPCQDs/ZnO (NPCZ) nanohybrid as efficient and robust photocatalysts for light-driven hydrogen production. The synthesized NPCZ catalyst exhibited improvement in hydrogen production of 417 μmolh−1g−1 under visible light compared to nitrogen doped CQDs/ZnO (NCZ), phosphorus doped CQDs/ZnO (PCZ) and CQDs/ZnO (CZ) nanohybrid photocatalysts. The synergistic interactions between NPCQDs and ZnO nanorods give rise to the formation of additional energy levels, decrease in recombination rate and increase in decay lifetime of photo-generated electron hole pairs, reduced work function, thus the eventual improved hydrogen generation performance in visible region. Further, the experimentally obtained results are consistently corroborated by the first principles Density Functional Theory (DFT), revealing the decrease in bandgap as well as work function and improvement in density of states (DOS) of NPCZ photocatalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic production of hydrogen from splitting of water has been evolved as a promising technology to address the global energy crunch and environmental issues due to its eco-friendliness, zero pollution, and high energy storage capacity [1–3]. Since the discovery of the photocatalytic process by Fujishima and Honda [4], various research groups have done extensive research on photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen generation using different materials such as nitrides [5], sulfides [6], oxysulfides [7], carbon nitrides [8], metal oxides [9], etc. Among all the metal oxide based photocatalysts, ZnO has a wide bandgap of 3.30–3.38 eV at 270 C and 60 meV as binding energy (BE) for free excitons [10], which makes it as an attractive candidate for applications in various field such as biological, biomedical, energy and photocatalysis [11]. Nevertheless, ZnO has an intrinsic limitation as it is active only in UV excitation and only 3 % of the UV light reaching the earth’s surface from sunlight[12]. Therefore, many approaches have been advanced to enhance the visible light absorption capacity of ZnO to harvest solar energy, such as loading noble metals [13], sensitization with organic dyes [14], doping with metals and non-metals [15], coupling with narrow band gap semiconductors [16] and with metal-free carbonaceous materials, such as C60 [17], carbon nanotubes [18], graphene [19,20] and carbon quantum dots [21].

Among all carbon-based materials, carbon quantum dots have evolved as novel emerging star of carbon family due to their distinctive optoelectronic, magnetic, and chemical properties which enable their applications in various fields including biosensors, bioimaging, biomedicine, electronics and renewable energy [22]. Unlike the chalcogenide semiconductor based quantum dots, CQDs are relatively less harmful and also reasonable to synthesize. CQDs are considered the potential candidate for optoelectronics, photovoltaics, and photocatalyst application due to their visible light-harvesting capability, electron-accepting, and electron-donating properties [22,23]. Nonetheless, the photocatalytic properties of CQDs can be further improved by doping nitrogen [24], phosphorus [25], boron [26], and sulfur [27]. Recent, reports have demonstrated an enhancement in the photocatalytic properties of carbon quantum dots co-doped with nitrogen and phosphorus [28], nitrogen and boron [29], nitrogen and sulfur [30] etc.

In the context of photocatalytic hydrogen production, various researchers have developed different photocatalysts based on ZnO nanostructures [31–36]. Ahmad et al., [31] reported cerium doped ZnO/carbon nanotube nanocomposite which were synthesized using hydrothermal deposition, one-pot hydrothermal and sol-gel, methods. They argued that the nanocomposite synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method showed the highest hydrogen production of 759 μmolh−1g−1 due to the small crystallite size, high surface area, low recombination rate as well as low diffusion pathway of photo-induced electrons, while the photocatalysts prepared by hydrothermal deposition and sol-gel method showed hydrogen production of 621 μmolh−1g−1 and 579 μmolh−1g−1, respectively. The hydrogen generation by photocatalytic activity of ZnO/CdS nanostructure was studied by Zhao et al., [32] and they observed the highest hydrogen production of 1725 μmolh−1g−1 compared to individual ZnO and CdS components. Zhao et al., [33] have also prepared ZnO/CdS nanocomposite by the facile and cost-effective method which revealed better catalytic activity in sunlight with the hydrogen production of 1807 μmolh−1g−1 due to electronic hybridization of ZnO band structures with ZnS surface states, the band bending and the surface dipole moment of ZnO. The multi-component hetero-nanostructure based on CuxO/ZnO@Au was reported by Zhou et al., [34] which exhibited enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of 12.4 μmolcm−2h−1 with superior stability and recyclability due to treelike hetero-nanostructure ensuring the transfer of photogenerated electrons to ZnO nanodisks in a controlled manner, while holes are transferred to CuO nanowires with combined efforts from Au plasmon, multi-node p-n junctions and polar ZnO facets. Li et al., [35] have reported the ZnO/CdSe-diethylenetriamine produced hydrogen of 11.09 molh−1g−1, which is 123.22 times greater than ZnO. They argued that the increased hydrogen generation of ZnO/CdSe-diethylenetriamine was due to the large specific surface area, wide range of light absorption, enhanced separation, and migration of photoinduced electrons. The photocatalytic hydrogen generation of boron doped C3N4/ZnO was studied by Kim et al., [36] and it produced hydrogen of 357 μmolg−1h−1, which is 2.9 fold times higher than the undoped C3N4/ZnO due to increased surface area and improved charge transfer through a cascade band structure.

In addition to this, various researchers have applied carbon quantum dots and ZnO based nanostructures for different applications. Among them, nitrogen, and sulfur doped CQDs/ZnO was used as solar cell [37], ZnO decorated with nitrogen and phosphorus doped CQDs for methylene blue degradation [38], nitrogen doped CQDs-ZnO hybrid structure for waste water treatment [39], CQDs sensitized ZnO:Ga/ZnO for photoelectrochemical water splitting [40], CQDs/ZnO nanorods as ultraviolet photodetector [41], CQDs decorated ZnO as antibacterial agent [42], CQDs supported ZnO sphere for dye degradation [43]. Nonetheless, there is a lacuna in the literature on NPCQDs anchored ZnO nanorods for photocatalytic hydrogen production and the validation of experimentally obtained results with first-principle density functional theory (DFT) simulations.

Therefore, in this research work, we developed NPCQDs/ZnO nanorods as visible light hydrogen production photocatalyst and compared their performance with NCZ nanohybrid, PCZ nanohybrid, CZ hybrid and the individual components. The developed photocatalysts were characterized by ultraviolet-visible absorbance spectroscopy, X-ray diffractogram (XRD), High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL). Also, the detailed energy band mechanism has been proposed using Mott-Schottky curves and Ultra-violet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) results, which is further corroborated by first-principle DFT simulations.

Glucose, orthophosphoric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA), zinc nitrate hexahydrate, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and all other compounds were of laboratory grade procured from Sigma and quantified amount of double distilled water (DDW) was used during the experiment.

The nitrogen and phosphorus codoped carbon quantum dots were synthesized using microwave-assisted method and the procedure is reported elsewhere [44]. The graphical representation of the preparation of NPCQDs is shown in Scheme 1(a). The as-prepared NPCQDs were anchored onto ZnO nanorods by hydrothermal method. In brief, solution A containing 1 M of Zinc nitrate hexahydrate was dissolved in 25 ml of DDW. Then, the different amount of NPCQDs (such as 0.5 wt%, 1.5 wt% 2.5 wt%, 5 wt%, 7.5 wt%) were added and stirred it for 15 min. Solotion B containing 2 M of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) in 25 ml of DDW was stirred it for 15 min. Solution B was added to solution A dropwise under constant stirring until it forms yellow color precipitate. Then, it was transfered to teflon lined autoclaves and heated at 200 °C for 2 h in an hot air oven and it was allowed cool down to ambient temperature. The obtained solution was filtrated using 0.22 µm filter paper and then dried at 70 °C for 24 h and named as (0.5 wt%) NPCQDs/ZnO nanorods (0.5 wt% NPCZ). Similarly, 1.5–7.5 wt% of NPCQDs were anchored on to ZnO nanorods separately, and abbreviated as 1.5 wt% NPCZ, 2.5 wt% NPCZ, 5 wt% NPCZ and 7.5 wt% NPCZ respectively. For the camparison, differenct weight percentage of PCQDs, NCQDs, and CQDs with different wieght percentage such as 1.5–7.5 wt% were anchored on to ZnO nanorods using above procedure. The graphical representation of synthesis of NPCZ nanohybrid is shown in Scheme 1(b).

UV-Visible absorption spectroscopy analyses for all the nanohybrids was carried out using JASCO, V-670 UV-Visible spectrophotometer. The particle size, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and elemental mapping of NSCT nanohybrid was studied using HRTEM of model JEOL/JEM 2100 at 200 kV. The Bruker AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation was used to study the crystal structure of the as-synthesized nanohybrid with wavelength 1.5406 Å. The acquired diffraction data were matched with reference patterns from the JCPDS database. The chemical nature of the samples was studied by Fourier transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) using the JASCO FTIR-6100 spectrometer and the Raman spectrum was aquaired with Raman spectrophotometer of the model Renishaw with laser excitation wavelength of 532 nm. XPS was carried out to analyze the chemical state, chemical bonding, and elemental composition of nanohybrids using Omicron EA-125 XPS analyzer at normal temperature. PL studies were carried out with Perkin Emler, LS-55 at normal atmospiric conditions. TRPL was performed with Horiba Jobin-Yvon IBH, UK and 372 nm pulsed nano light emitting diode was used as light source. UPS was performed with Indus-1 synchrotron source at AIPES beamline (BL-02). The photoelectrochemical (PEC) experiments were performed with a three-electrode glass cell linked to an electrochemical workstation equipped with a 150 W Xenon light. All the nanohybrids along with ZnO were used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and platinum wire as the counter electrode in 1 M KOH electrolyte. The photocurrent response was measured by illuminating the photoanodes under 150 W Xenon lamp at the ambient condition in a dark chamber. Further, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed using the same electrochemical workstation in the frequency range from 1 MHz to 0.01 Hz.

The photocatalytic generation of hydrogen experiment was performed using 300 W Xenon lamp. 0.02 g of NPCZ photocatalyst was added to Pyrex glass photoreactor containing 20 ml aqueous solution of sodium sulfide (0.2 M) and sodium sulfite (0.1 M) in which it acts as sacrificial agent. The solution present in the photoreactor was degassed and exposed to light from a Xenon lamp (with Ultra-violet cut-off filter λ ≥ 410 nm at ambient temperature and pressure. The photoreactor was maintained at ambient temperature by circulating water continuously. The amount of hydrogen generated was determined with online gas chromatograph (GCMS) connected to thermal conductivity detector and 5 Å molecular sieve column. Similar kind of experimental procedure was followed for PCZ, NCZ and CZ nanohybrids to record hydrogen generation. The stability of NPCZ photocatalyst over 16 h was carried out.

The DFT calculations were performed within the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) [45,46]. The interactions between the valence and core electrons were treated using the project-augmented-wave (PAW) technique. Long-range van der Waals (vdW) interactions were accounted for all calculations via the Grimme method (DFT-D3) [47]. A kinetic energy cut-off of 500 eV and a Monkhorst–Pack [48] k-point 7 × 7 × 5 mesh were used to model the Brillouin zone of ZnO. The conjugate gradient algorithm was used for the optimizations of geometry until the residual Hellmann–Feynman forces on all relaxed atoms reached 0.01 eV Å–1and Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation functional was used to determine the electronic exchange–correlation potential [49]. The most stable ZnO (101̅0) surface [50] was considered for the construction of the nanohybrids with the pristine and doped CQDs. The CQDs were modelled by using hexagonal graphene flake in a 25 × 25 × 25 Å3 of orthorhombic dimension, containing seven hexagons with edge C atoms saturated with hydrogen atoms [51,52]. The work function (WF) was determined using the relation WF = E vacuum – E F, where E vacuum and E F are the vacuum level and Fermi level, respectively [53–55]. Dipole correction perpendicular to the surface was described, which confirmed that there is no net dipole perpendicular to the surfaces that may impact on the potential in the vacuum level.

The ultraviolet-visible absorbance spectra of the nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and phosphorus doped carbon quantum dost (PCQDs) series and CQDs are shown in Fig. S1 (a & b) respectively. The spectra depicted a prominent peak in ultreviolet region and shoulder peak in visible region due to π-π * transition and n-π * transition, respectively. Also, it is noteworthy to observe that the spectra showed a redshift in the absorption edge till 15 at% in case of NCQDs and 7.5 at% in case of PCQDs, and then displayed a blue shift. The red shift in the absorption edge revealed the decrement in the bandgap and blue shift in the absorption edge revealed the increment in the bandgap [56]. Since NCQDs (15 at%N) and PCQDs (7.5 at%P) showed less bandgap, hence the 15 at% nitrogen and 7.5 at% phosphorus was used to co-dope for CQDs to prepare NPCQDs.

The obtained UV-Visible absorbance spectra of the NPCQDs is depected in Fig. 1(a). It exhibits a prominent peak in the ultra violet region (π-π * transition) due to sp2 aromatic carbon as well as a small peak in the visible region (n-π * transition) due to CO groups [56]. Fig. 1(b) represents the UV-Visible absorbance spectra of NPCZ series with different loading of NPCQDs along with ZnO nanorods. The obtained spectra of ZnO nanorods exhibited absorption edge at 381 nm. Nonetheless, this absorption edge gets redshifted for (2.5 wt%) NPCZ revealing a decrease in the bandgap. Further, the bangap of the NPCZ series was calculated by using Tauc’s plot. Fig. 1(c) shows the tauc’s plot of the NPCZ series. the bandgap of ZnO was found to be 3.23 eV and it reduced to 2.71 eV for 2.5 wt% NPCZ. The UV-Visible absorbance spectra and corresponding Tauc’s plots of NCZ, PCZ, and CZ series are displayed in Fig. S2 (a & d), S2 (b & e), and S2 (c & f) respectively. It can be observed from Fig. S2 that the PCZ series showed less bandgap compared to the NCZ and CZ series due to higher DOS provided from phosphorus [28]. The less band gap was observed for 2.50 wt% NPCZ, 2.50 wt% NCZ, 2.50 wt% PCZ and 2.50 wt% CZ, hence they are considered for further discussions.

The size and surface morphology of NPCZ nanohybrid was studied using HRTEM. Fig. 2(a) shows the NPCQDs of size (2−5) nm anchored onto the ZnO nanorod of diameter (25−40) nm and length ∼70 nm. The HRTEM image of NPCZ (Fig. 2(c)) depicts d-spacing of 2.1 Å corresponding to (100) plane of NPCQDs and 2.42 Å corresponding to (101) plane of ZnO nanorods supporting XRD results which is described in next section. The selected area diffraction pattern of NPCZ (displayed as inset of Fig. 2(c)) exhibited (002), (101) and (110) planes revealing the structure of ZnO nanorods as hexagonal wurtzite.

The chemical composition and elemental distribution of NPCZ nanohybrid was investigated by EDX spectroscopy. The EDX analysis and elemental mapping images (N, P, C, Zn, O) of NPCZ nanohybrids are depicted in Fig. S3. The EDX analysis confirmed that the sample is comprised of N, P, C, Zn and O elements with an average atomic percentage ratio of 0.58, 0.26, 3.40, 65.32 and 27.04 respectively for NPCZ nanohybrid (Fig. S3(a)), Cu mesh is utilized during EDX analysis, which results in the formation of a copper peak. Further. EDX mapping images (Fig. S3(b-f)) confirm that all the atoms were evenly distributed in the nanohybrid indicating that formation of heterojunction between NPCQDs and ZnO nanorods.

XRD pattern of ZnO, CZ, NCZ, PCZ, and NPCZ nanohybrids is shown in Fig. 3(a). The ZnO nanorods showed peaks corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112) and (201) planes and is in good agreement with JCPDS-05-0664 which confirms the structure of ZnO nanorods as hexagonal wurtzite [57]. These peaks also appeared in the CZ, NCZ, PCZ and NPCZ nanohybrids indicating the presence of ZnO nanorods. Nevertheless, the intensity of these peaks decreased slightly in the nanohybrids due to the anchoring of quantum dots on them. The absence of NPCQDs peaks is due to their less concentration. In order to determine the chemical properties of the nanohybrids, XPS analyses was carried out and its survey scan is depicted in Fig. 3(b). It exhibited the peaks at 1022 eV, 532 eV, 398 eV, 285 eV and 133 eV attributed to Zn2p, O1s, N1s, C1s and P2p revealing the presence NPCQDs and ZnO in NPCZ. Fig. 3(c) displays the high-resolution XPS of C1s of NPCZ, which is de-convoluted into two peaks i.e. 285.73 eV due to C-C functional group and 287.84 eV due to CO functional group. The high resolution XPS of N1s of NPCZ (depicted in Fig. 3(d)) is deconvoluted into four peaks, i.e. 397.95 eV due to C-N-C, 398.83 eV due to N-(C)3, 399.85 eV due to pyridine like N and 401.3 eV due to pyrrolic like N. Similarly, Fig. 3(e) shows the deconvoluted high resolution XPS of O1s of NPCZ which is decovaluted into three peaks at 530.38 eV corresponding to Zn-O, 531.51 eV corresponding to Zn-OH and 533.22 eV corresponding to CO bonds. Fig. 3(f) shows the highresolution XPS of P2p which is deconvoluted into two peaks, i.e. 133.2 eV due to P2p3/2 and 134.2 eV due to P2p1/2. The high resolution XPS of Zn2p of ZnO, CZ, NCZ, PCZ, and NPCZ is shown in Fig. 3(g), which is deconvoluted into two peaks, i.e. a peak at 1022 eV due to Zn2p3/2 and another peak at 1045 eV due to Zn2P1/2 for ZnO nanorods. Also, it can be observed that these peaks were shifted towards higher binding energy for NPCZ compared to ZnO nanorods indicating the interaction of anchored NPCQDs with ZnO nanorods [31]. Fig. 3(h) shows the FTIR spectra of CZ, NCZ, PCZ, and NPCZ nanohybrids along with ZnO nanorods. All the samples displayed a peak around 3456 cm−1 corresponds to -OH stretching vibrations [37]. The spectra showed peaks at 2962 cm−1 and 2898 cm−1 corresponds to C-H stretching vibrational mode which are present in quantum dots. The peaks at 1093 cm−1 and 894 cm−1 are attributed with the C-O bonds from quantum dots [58]. The peaks observed at 1456 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1 are due to the vibration of the aromatic ring in quantum dots [58]. The characteristic peaks at 1238 cm−1, 1462 cm−1, 1573 cm−1 and 1639 cm−1 are corresponding to stretching vibration modes of C-N heterocycles and peak at 3248 cm−1 is indicative of NH stretching vibrations [59]. The characteristic peaks at 419 cm−1 and 563 cm−1 are attributed to Zn-O stretching vibrations [42]. The actual active sites for hydrogen production activity are carbon atoms next to oxygen-containing groups, and their high catalytic activity is primarily the result of synergistic catalysis between carboxyl (COOH) and other types of oxygen-containing groups (namely, hydroxyl (OH), epoxide (C-O-C), and carbonyl (CO)) next to each other on NPCQDs[60].

The Raman spectra of ZnO along with different nanohybrids are shown in Fig. 3(i). All the spectra exhibited peaks around 199 cm−1 and 438 cm−1 respectively corresponding to E2(low) and E2(high) mode of non-polar optical phonons associated with wurtzite ZnO phase. The vibrational modes observed around 580 cm−1 attributed to E1(L0) mode of ZnO and is shifted slightly towards higher order for nanohybrids attributing the possible interaction of quantum dots with ZnO nanorods via oxygen functional groups. In addition to these peaks, nanohybrids exhibited two more peaks around 1362 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 respectively corresponding to D band and G band respectively. The D band arises due to the k-point breathing mode of A1 g phonons, and G band arises due to the tangential stretching mode of E2 g phonons of sp2 atoms [61]. The appearance of Raman characteristics peaks of ZnO and carbon quantum dots confirm their presence in nanohybrids. The Raman spectroscopy along with HR-TEM, EDX, XRD, XPS and FTIR spectroscopy confirmed the successful nchoring of NPCQDs on ZnO nanorods.

Fig. S4 (a) and S4 (b) shows the PL spectra of NCQDs and PCQDs series, respectively. It can be seen from the figure that the PL main peak shifted towards higher wavelength with addition of nitrogen and phosphorus concentration in CQDs for 15 at% nitrogen and 7.5 at% phosphorus and blue-shifted thereafter. This is due to the doping of phosphorus and nitrogen causing the introduction of electrons on the surface of the CQDs. This changes the electronic atmosphere of CQDs tuning the fluorescence intensity towards visible region. Nevertheless, surface passivation, radiative recombination of excitons, triplet zigzag ends, and HOMO-LUMO energy gap, are also liable for the inherent fluorescence and shifting toward its emission to visible region [62]. The photoluminoscene quantum yield was determined to be 0.69 for NP-CQDs at 360 nm excitation wavelength, which is in good agreement with that of previously reported CQDs value (Fig. S5) [63]. Fig. 4(a) depicts the PL emission spectra of NPCZ, NCZ, PCZ, CZ and ZnO nanorods obtained at 320 nm excitation wavelength. The PL spectra of ZnO nanorods exhibited two peaks, a peak centered on 382 nm arises due to free excitons recombination and a main peak centered on 550 nm arises due to transition from oxygen vacancy level [40,64,65]. The PL intensity of both the peaks decreased for quantum dots anchored nanohybrids and found to be very less for NPCZ nanohybrids signifying the suppressed recombination rate further increasing the carrier lifetime of the photo-generated electron and hole pairs in the nanohybrids. Therefore, the decay lifetime of charge carriers for the nanohybrids along with ZnO nanorods had been determined using the time-resolved PL spectroscopy. Fig. 4(b) shows the TRPL spectra of as-synthesized nanohybrid along with ZnO. The average decay lifetime of charge carrier for ZnO nanorods was found to be 1.64 ns and it increased to 3.2 ns, 5.1 ns, 6.4 ns and 7.9 ns for CZ, NCZ, PCZ and NPCZ nanohybrids respectively. The increase in the charge carrier lifetime of the NPCZ arises due to the electronic transition of the intrinsic core (sp2 conjugated π transition) and the surface states present in quantum dots [66]. Such distinctive and longer fluorescence life-times of quantum dots might be responsible for the improvement in photocatalytic hygrogen generation activity in NPCZ nanohybrid. Where τ is the average decay time of the nanohybrids and ϕ
D
0 is the quantum yield. The κ
r were observed to be 21.25 × 107 s−1, 13.33 × 107 s−1, 10.62 × 107 s−1 and 8.6 × 107 s−1 for CZ, NCZ, PCZ and NPCZ respectively. The κ
nr were found to be 10 × 107 s−1, 6.2 × 107 s−1, 5 × 107 s−1 and 5.05 × 107 s−1 for CZ. NCZ, PCZ and NPCZ respectively. The calculated rate constants implies that the radiative recombination leads in these nanohybrids. It can be noticed that the NPCZ showed more decay lifetime compared to PCZ, NCZ and CZ due to nitrogen (n-type) and phosphorus doping (n-type) in CQDs because it promotes the radiative relaxation route by generating virtual energy levels above the valence band, which impacts the average decay time. Moreover, these virtually formed energy levels provided from NPCQDs may induce the shifting of fermi energy level near the conduction band(CB) of ZnO thereby decrease in the WF Therefore, in order to study work function and shift in the fermi energy ZnO UPS was caried out for all the nanohybrids along with ZnO. Fig. 4(c) and (d) shows the secondary edge and fermi edge of UPS for nanohybrids and ZnO nanorods. It can be observed from Fig. 4(d) that the Fermi edge moved towards vacuum level for carbon quantum dots anchored ZnO nanorods implying that the decrease in work function (ϕ) of nanohybrids. The WF was calculated using the equation [28],
(3)
ϕ = h ν −
|
E
sec
−
E
F
|
where hν is incident photon energy, E
sec is the secondary emission onset, E
EF is Fermi edge. The calculated work function was found to be 4.89, 4.02, 3.51, 3.25 and 2.89 eV respectively for ZnO, CZ, NCZ, PCZ and NPCZ nanohybrids. The work function of nanohybrids was found to be decreased compared to ZnO nanorods alone indicating the easy generation of electrons under visible light thereby enhancing the photocatalytic hydrogen generation activity. It is worth to notice that NPCZ showed more decrement in work function due to the intermediate energy levels formed by free electrons of phosphorus and nitrogen dopants in CQDs. Hencee more improvement in the visible light photocatalytic generation of hydrogen can be expected from NPCZ nanohybrids.

These results were corroborated via first-principles DFT calculations, wherein the lowest-energy heterostructures of the CQDs/ZnO (CZ), N-CQDs/ZnO (NCZ), P-CQDs/ZnO (PCZ), and NP-CQDs/ZnO (NPCZ) were characterized as shown in Fig. 5. In all nanohybrids, the CQDs display bending geometry after energy minimization, such that the hydrogen atoms are oriented towards the surface oxygen atoms. This indicates strong interaction between CQDs and the ZnO (100) surface. Consistently with observe electron density accumulation within the interaction regions as revealed by the differential charge density isosurface plots (Fig. 5. a2-d2)). The interfacial adhesion energy per unit area (E ad) was calculated to ascertain the adsorption stability of the CQDs on ZnO(100) surface and is defined as: E ad = (E interface - E CQDs - E ZnO(100))/S where E interface, E CQDs, and E ZnO(100) are the total energies of the interface with surface area S, CQDs, and ZnO(100) surface, respectively. S the slab surface area of the interface. The adhesion energy for the CQDs/ZnO, N-CQDs/ZnO, P-CQDs/ZnO (PCZ), and NP-CQDs/ZnO (NPCZ) nanohybrids is calculated at − 0.11, − 0.13, − 0.12, and − 0.17 V/Å2, all of which are negative, indicating the formation of stable interfaces. The predicted interfacial structures of the nanohybrids suggest very weak interactions between the carbon quantum dots (CQD) and the ZnO surface, with the CQDs stabilized mainly via weak hydrogen-bonded (1.957–2.541 Å) and van der Waals interactions. The vdW contribution to the adhesion energy for the CQDs/ZnO, N-CQDs/ZnO, P-CQDs/ZnO (PCZ), and NP-CQDs/ZnO (NPCZ) nanohybrids is calculated at − 0.08, − 0.10, − 0.08, and − 0.07 V/Å2, respectively. These results indicate that the dominant type of bonding at the CQDs/ZnO interphase is through vdW interactions with small contributions coming from weak hydrogen-bonded interactions, except for the NP-CQDs/ZnO composite wherein P-O covalent bonding dominates (−0.10 eV). Because of the electronic interactions between the CQDs and the ZnO (100) surface, we found in a decrease in the WF for the nanohybrids (CZ = 4.16 eV, NCZ = 3.67 eV, PCZ = 3.39 eV, and NPCZ = 3.01 eV) compared to the isolated ZnO(100) surface (WF = 4.93 eV) shown in Fig. 6(a–d). There is generally a good agreement between measured and calculated value and trend of the work function for each material. A gradual shift in the Fermi level (E F) towards the conduction band is observed the E F calculated at − 1.14, − 0.63, 0.32, − 0.02 eV for the CZ, NCZ, PCZ, and NPCZ nanohybrids. The decreased work function of the NPCZ nanohybrids is expected to facilitate electron transfer processes and thus lead to enhance photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic hydrogen production of CQDs, NCQDs, PCQDs, NPCQDs, CZ, NCZ, PCZ and NPCZ photocatalysts was carried out along with ZnO nanorods and is shown in Fig. 7(a). It can be seen from figure that the ZnO has produced hydrogen of 34 μmolh−1g−1. Nonetheless, NPCQDs showed hydrogen production of 186 μmolh−1g−1 which was even more compared to CQDs, NCQDs and PCQDs. The increase in the amount of hydrogen generation of NPCQDs was due to the addition of energy levels provided by phosphorus and nitrogen dopants to CQDs which results in Fermi energy level (Ef) shift towards conduction band of NPCQDs. Thus, more number of electrons were excited from valance band to conduction band of NPCQDs which helps to improve photocatalytic activity [33]. It is worth to notice that the photocatalytic hydrogen production was found to be 332 μmolh−1g−1, 372 μmolh−1g−1, 391 μmolh−1g−1 and 417 μmolh−1g−1 for CZ, NCZ, PCZ and NPCZ photocatalysts respectively. Further, hydrogen production activity of the NPCZ series with different loading of NPCQDs along with ZnO nanorods are shown Fig. S6(a). The hydrogen production activity of 2.5 wt% NPCZ showed maximum hydrogen production as compared to other nanohybrids(0.5 wt% NPCZ, 1.5 wt% NPCZ, 5 wt% NPCZ).

The transient photocurrent responses of ZnO, CZ, NCZ, PCZ, and NPCZ photoanodes were studied using chronoamperometric measurements under chopped illumination circumstances at a constant voltage of 0.8 V, as shown in Fig. S6(c). Because of its lowest bandgap, NPCZ nanohybrids demonstrated a maximum transient photocurrent (260 μA cm−2) compared to PCZ (178 μA cm−2), NCZ(87 μA cm−2), CZ(62 μA cm−2), and ZnO (33 μA cm−2). This suggests an improved interfacial charge transfer kinetics between the NPCZ and the electrolyte, as well as effective charge carrier separation, which would contributes to improved water splitting performance [68]. The EIS was used to investigate the interfacial charge transfer resistance (Rct) between the electrolyte and the nanohybrids along with ZnO. Fig. S6(d) shows the Nyquist plots obtained for CZ, NCZ, PCZ, NPCZ nanohybrids along with ZnO photoanodes under visible light illumination. The diameter of the semicircle of the EIS curve (corresponding to interfacial charge transfer resistance) for NPCZ nanohybrid was much smaller than that of PCZ, NCZ, CZ nanohybrids and ZnO, implying an efficient charge transfer with lower charge transfer resistance between the photoanode and the electrolyte [69]. This shows quicker charge transfer and greater charge separation at the interface of NPCZ nanohybrid when compared to other nanohybrids, owing to a reduction in surface recombination. The reduced recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers in NPCZ is further supported by the PL spectroscopic observations detailed above.

The improved photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of NPCZ photocatalyst compared to other photocatalysts can be described by using the energy band diagram as depicted in Fig. 7(b). The Mott-Schottky (MS) plots were recorded (depicted in Fig. 7(c)) to propose the mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen generation of NPCZ photocatalyst. It can be seen from Fig. 7(c) that M-S plots of ZnO, NPCZ and NPCQD depicted the positive slope revealing its behavior as n-type semiconductor. The extrapolated line of M-S plots to X-axis gives the flat band potential, to the obtained flat band potion values are − 0.937 V, − 1.107 V and − 1.185 V (v/s Ag/AgCl) for ZnO and NPCZ respectively. These flat band potentials are corresponding to − 0.74 eV, − 0.91 eV and − 0.99 eV (v/s NHE) for ZnO, NPCZ and NPCQDs respectively, which could be measured as bottom value of conduction band (ECBb). It is noteworthy to observe that the flat band potential of NPCZ photocatalyst shifted to negative side representing that improvement in the capabity to reduce the electrons which in turn increases the photocatalytic generation of hydrogen [46,47]. The maximum energy level of the valence band (E
VBm ) can be determined using [48]:

, where E
g is band gap. The valence band values(EVBm) found to be 2.49 eV and 1.39 eV (v/s NHE) for ZnO and NPCQDs respectively, The energy difference (E

, and they were determined to be − 0.48 eV and − 0.52 eV for ZnO and NPCZ photocatalysts respectively. The schematic mechanism of electron transition within the NPCZ photocatalyst for hydrogen production can be explained using the mechanism as shown in Scheme 2. The quantum dots in NPCZ catalyst absorb light and generates the photoexcited electrons in its conduction band. As the conduction band offset of NPCQDs is higher than that of ZnO, so these photogenerated electrons are transferred from conduction band of NPCQDs to conduction band of ZnO nanorods. Further, ZnO also generates photoexcited electrons in its conduction band under light. These photogenerated electrons from both NPCQDs and ZnO reduces the H+ to H2 as shown in Scheme 2, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic performance. Also, time resolved PL spectroscopic measurements revealed the increase in lifetime of photogenerated charge carriers thereby providing the sufficient time for photogenerated electrons to reduce H+ to H2 which was further supported by decrease in the suppression of recombination of photogenerated charge carriers as studied by PL spectroscopy. The hydrogen produced by the developed photocatalyst has been compared with that of reported results and are tabulated in Table S1. The hydrogen generation performance of nobel metal loaded ZnO nanohybrid is high as compared to NPCZ. However, the carbon quantum dots based photocatlysts are relatively cheaper than that of the nobel metal based photocatlysts. The obtained results suggest the NPCQDs scaffiolded ZnO nanohybrids increases the hydrogen production activity about ∼10 times higher than that of ZnO. The, improved hydrogen production activity of ZnO using NPCQDs are extendable to other semiconductor based nanohybrids using NPCQDs to enhance their hydrogen production efficiency using visible-light.

The stability of the NPCZ photocatalyst was investigated over 16 h as it depicts the better performance of hydrogen generation under visible light and is displayed in Fig. 5(c). The photocatalyst exhibited exceptional stability without much noticeable variation in hydrogen production. In order to study the effect of photocatalytic activity on structure of NPCZ catalyst, it was recovered after 16 h, cleaned with double distilled water and ethanol, dried at room temperature. Then, XRD was performed for obtained powder and it is shown in Fig. S7. It can be seen from XRD pattern of NPCZ photocatalyst that the peaks were similar to that of NPCZ photocatalyst before photocatalytic activity, but the intensity of diffraction peaks decreased. The enhanced visible light photocatalytic hydrogen production of NPCZ makes it as a promising catalyst in its class.

The NPCZ photocatalyst was developed for visible light hydrogen production and it exhibited enhanced hydrogen production performance compared to PCZ, NCZ and CZ photocatalysts. The enhanced visible light hydrogen production of NPCZ was due to the synergistic effects of NPCQDs and ZnO nanorods such as (i) suppressed recombination centers as studied by PL spectroscopy; (ii) increased lifetime of charge carriers as demonstrated by time resolved PL spectroscopy; (iii) decrease in work function as revealed by UPS spectroscopy and DFT simulations and (iv) increase in density of states as observed by DFT simulations. Also, NPCZ demonstrated the excellent stability for hydrogen production. We can conclude from this that NPCZ is a promising photocatalyst for the visible light hydrogen production.
